127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.

135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137

1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	
4	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
5	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
6	
7	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
8	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
9	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
10	\[
11	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
12	  \qquad
13	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
14	\]
15	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
16	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
17	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
18	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
19	
20	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
21	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
22	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
23	% groups)
24	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
25	% SOURCE QUOTE:
26	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
27	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
28	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
29	% abelian groups defined by the formula
30	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
31	% \end{dfn}
32	%  [\dots]
33	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
34	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
35	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
36	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
37	% topologies  by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
39	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
40	%  \end{dfn}"
41	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
42	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
43	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
44	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
45	homomorphisms.}
46	
47	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
48	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
49	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
50	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
51	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
52	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
53	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
54	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
55	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
56	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
57	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
58	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
59	structure. This is the content of
60	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
61	
62	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
63	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
64	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
65	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
66	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
67	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
68	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
69	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
70	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
71	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
72	\begin{enumerate}
73	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
74	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
75	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
76	    isomorphism classes;
77	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
78	    for \(\otimes\);
79	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
80	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
81	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
82	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
83	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
84	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
85	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
86	\end{enumerate}
87	At Mathlib's pinned commit, the entire monoidal scaffolding for (3) is already
88	available abstractly, and the project's task is to \emph{instantiate} it rather
89	than to hand-assemble the coherence data. Two facts drive this. First, the
90	category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a
91	presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric) monoidal category in
92	Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)), with the sectionwise
93	relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)\); this applies
94	verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf \(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second,
95	Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
96	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
97	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
98	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
99	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\) --- associator,
100	left/right unitors, braiding, and \emph{all} coherence (pentagon, triangle,
101	naturalities) derived. Sheafification of presheaves of modules is exactly the
102	localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the locally-bijective class
103	\(W = J.W\), so the monoidal structure on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) is
104	\emph{free} from this API once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) is supplied.
105	
106	Consequently the only genuinely-new obligation is the single instance
107	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) for the sheafification localizer on
108	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\). Its two fields are the
109	left/right whiskering-stability statements \(J.W\,g \Rightarrow J.W(F \triangleleft
110	g)\) and \(J.W\,g \Rightarrow J.W(g \triangleright F)\) for an \emph{arbitrary}
111	module \(F\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}), and these are flatness-free: a
112	\(J.W\)-morphism is a stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x =
113	\mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\) is again an isomorphism (tensoring with an
114	isomorphism preserves isomorphisms --- no flatness, no local triviality). The
115	load-bearing half is the local-injectivity statement
116	\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, whose stalkwise proof needs two
117	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\)-level ingredients that are Mathlib-absent at the
118	pinned commit (the module-level stalk characterisation of \(J.W\), and the
119	commutation of the stalk with the relative tensor); this is the
120	\emph{sole remaining open obligation} for the whole group-law engine.
121	
122	With \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) in hand, the substrate \(\otimes_X\) carries the
123	associator, unitors and braiding from \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\), and the
124	group law assembles the commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes whose units
125	group is the Picard group (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}), exactly as
126	Mathlib builds \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) as
127	\(\mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\). The
128	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},

131	\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) and any
132	separate tensor-inverse lemma are \emph{off} this critical path; see also

274	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
275	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
276	
277	\begin{definition}
278	
279	\leanok
280	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
281	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
282	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
283	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
284	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
285	  \[
286	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
287	  \]
288	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
289	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
290	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
291	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
292	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
293	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
294	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
295	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
296	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
297	  canonical compatibility
298	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
299	     \;\cong\;
300	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
301	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
302	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
303	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
304	
305	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
306	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
307	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
308	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
309	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
310	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
311	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
312	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
313	\end{definition}
314	
315	\begin{lemma}
316	
317	\leanok
318	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
319	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
320	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
321	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
322	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
323	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
324	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
325	  \[
326	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
327	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
328	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
329	  \]
330	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
331	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
332	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
333	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
334	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
335	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
336	  \[
337	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
338	    \qquad
339	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
340	  \]
341	  the associator
342	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
343	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
344	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
345	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
346	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
347	\end{lemma}
348	
349	\begin{proof}
350	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
351	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
352	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
353	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
354	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
355	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
356	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
357	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
358	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
359	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
360	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
361	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
362	\end{proof}
363	
364	\begin{remark}
365	[The full monoidal category is cheap via route~(e); the group law consumes only propositions]
366	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
367	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
368	        lem:whisker_of_W}
369	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
370	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
371	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
372	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
373	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
374	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. Under route~(e) this is
375	  \emph{cheap}, not expensive: it is exactly the output of
376	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
377	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
378	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), once the single instance
379	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}) is supplied. All the
380	  coherence is then derived by the API.
381	
382	  Crucially, \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) needs \emph{no} closed structure. Its
383	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
384	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
385	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}: a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
386	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
387	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\) (tensoring with an isomorphism
388	  preserves isomorphisms). This is the same technique Mathlib uses for
389	  fixed-base presheaves in
390	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\), ported to
391	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\). It uses neither
392	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
393	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
394	  false over a non-affine open and is therefore off the critical path); the
395	  earlier reading that arbitrary-\(F\) whisker stability requires
396	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) was the \emph{wrong altitude}.
397	
398	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
399	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
400	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
401	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
402	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
403	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
404	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
405	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
406	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
407	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, all now supplied by the route~(e) monoidal structure;
408	  none of them is built from the off-path restriction-compatibility isomorphism
409	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group
410	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}),
411	  whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is transported from
412	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\): there
413	  over a fixed ring, here over the sheafification localizer via
414	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\).
415	\end{remark}

417	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
418	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
419	
420	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
421	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
422	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
423	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
424	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
425	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
426	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes ---
427	under route~(e) these are read off the \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure of
428	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} --- together with the closure of \(\otimes\) on the
429	locally-trivial \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype
430	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and the interaction with the pullback
431	functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion restriction-compatibility isomorphism
432	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is retained as an off-path supplement, not as a
433	group-law ingredient; cf.\ \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
434	
435	\begin{lemma}
436	
437	\leanok
438	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
439	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
440	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
441	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
442	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
443	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
444	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
445	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
446	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
447	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
448	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
449	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
450	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
451	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
452	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
453	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
454	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
455	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
456	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
457	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
458	  \[
459	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
460	      \;\;:=\;\;
461	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
462	  \]
463	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
464	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
465	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
466	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
467	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
468	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
469	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
470	\end{lemma}
471	
472	\begin{proof}
473	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
474	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
475	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
476	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
477	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
478	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
479	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
480	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
481	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
482	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
483	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
484	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
485	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
486	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
487	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
488	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
489	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
490	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
491	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
492	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
493	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
494	
495	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
496	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
497	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
498	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
499	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
500	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
501	\end{proof}
502	
503	\begin{lemma}
504	
505	\leanok
506	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
507	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
508	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
509	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
510	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
511	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
512	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
513	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
514	  morphism
515	  \[

522	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
523	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
524	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
525	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.

528	\begin{proof}
529	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
530	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
531	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
532	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
533	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
534	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
535	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
536	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
537	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
538	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
539	  isomorphism}
540	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
541	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
542	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
543	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
544	  therefore extension of scalars along
545	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.

547	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
548	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
549	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
550	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
551	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
552	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
553	  the abstract pullback functor.
554	
555	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
556	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
557	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
558	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
559	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
560	   \;\cong\;
561	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
562	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
563	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
564	  comparison
565	  \[
566	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
567	      \;\longrightarrow\;
568	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
569	      \;\otimes\;
570	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
571	  \]
572	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
573	
574	  \emph{Step 3 (off the critical path).} Completing this comparison at the
575	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise description of the abstract functor
576	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
577	  adjoint of pushforward and admits no sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the
578	  pinned commit. The genuine residual decomposes into two Mathlib-absent
579	  presheaf-level ingredients --- a presheaf-level identification

581	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) (needing a presheaf-level pushforward
582	  adjunction, only the sheaf version of which exists), and a strong-monoidal
583	  upgrade of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the ring isomorphism
584	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\). This lemma is therefore \emph{not} on the critical path
585	  for the relative Picard group law: the group law is built from
586	  \(\otimes\)-invertibility (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) and the
587	  coherence isomorphisms
588	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
589	  none of which consume restriction-compatibility. The statement is retained
590	  only as an optional supplement; its full proof is deferred.

659	\begin{proof}
660	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
661	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
662	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
663	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
664	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
665	
666	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
667	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
668	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
669	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
670	  \[
671	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
672	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
673	  \]
674	
675	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
676	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
677	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
678	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
679	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
680	
681	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
682	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
683	  linear maps (Mathlib
684	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
685	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
686	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
687	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).

690	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
691	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
692	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
693	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
694	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
695	\end{proof}

What is \emph{genuinely Mathlib-absent} at the pinned commit is narrower than a
total absence of stalk infrastructure. The \emph{stalk module structure} is
already present in Mathlib: the file
\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk} supplies, for a topological
space \(X\), a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) on \(X\) and a point
\(x \in X\), the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure on the stalk
\(\mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) of a presheaf of modules \(M\),
together with the germ--scalar compatibility \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\). What was
Mathlib-absent is only the \emph{linearity packaging} of the \emph{induced stalk
map} of a morphism of presheaves of modules --- that the map on stalks is
\(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear, characterised on germs, bijective when the
underlying \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map is an isomorphism, and bundled as a linear
equivalence. This packaging is now built project-side as the four declarations of
\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map} (the \emph{linearity half} of ingredient (d.1)). The
pieces that remain genuinely Mathlib-absent are: there is no monoidal
\(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\); the (d.1)-\emph{bridge} between the site-level
\(J.W\) and the topological stalkwise-isomorphism criterion on
\(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\); and (d.2) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
\otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk with the relative module-presheaf tensor. So
\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) for the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) sheafification
localizer must still be built project-side, and its two residual stalk-level
ingredients --- the (d.1)-bridge and (d.2) --- are ported by
\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. Once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
lands, the associator, unitors and braiding are \emph{free} from (ii) and the
bespoke three-step composite is superseded.

whiskering-stability lemmas below. Their load-bearing local-injectivity half is
the sole remaining open obligation of the entire group-law engine.

\begin{lemma}

[The $R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x$-linear stalk map of a morphism of presheaves of modules]
  \label{lem:stalk_linear_map}
  \lean{PresheafOfModules.stalkLinearMap, PresheafOfModules.stalkLinearMap_germ, PresheafOfModules.stalkLinearMap_bijective_of_isIso, PresheafOfModules.stalkLinearEquivOfIsIso}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(R\) a presheaf of commutative rings on
  \(X\), and \(x \in X\) a point. For a morphism \(g : M \to N\) of presheaves of
  \(R\)-modules, the induced map on stalks
  \[
    g_x \;\colon\; M_x \;\longrightarrow\; N_x
  \]
  is \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\)), and is
  characterised on germs by
  \(g_x(\mathrm{germ}_x\,s) = \mathrm{germ}_x(g_U\,s)\)
  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_germ}\)). When the underlying map of
  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-valued stalks is an isomorphism, \(g_x\) is bijective
  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\)); in that case it bundles to
  an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear equivalence
  \(M_x \xrightarrow{\sim} N_x\) (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearEquivOfIsIso}\)).

  These four declarations are the \emph{(d.1)-partial implementation} --- the
  linearity half of ingredient (d.1) of
  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} --- consumed by the
  \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step in the proof of that lemma. They build on the
  stalk module structure of \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk};
  the linearity packaging itself is project-original.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  The stalk \(M_x = \mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) carries its
  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure from
  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk}. The underlying additive map
  \(g_x\) on stalks is the filtered colimit of the section maps \(g_U\); since
  each section map is \(R(U)\)-linear and the scalar action on the stalk is the
  colimit of the section actions, \(g_x\) respects the
  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-action, which gives the linear map together with its
  germ characterisation. Bijectivity is read off the underlying
  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk isomorphism (a bijective linear map is a linear
  equivalence), and bundling the inverse yields the linear equivalence.
\end{proof}

The two ingredients this stalkwise computation needs are, at the pinned commit,
  absent from Mathlib at the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level, and constitute
  the genuine residual:
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item[(d.1)] the stalkwise characterisation of the module-level \(J.W\) on
      \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) (that \(g \in J.W\) is equivalent to \(g_x\) being an
      isomorphism for every \(x\)); and
    \item[(d.2)] the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x \otimes_{R_x}
      B_x\) of the stalk with the relative module-presheaf tensor, under which
      \((F \triangleleft g)_x\) is identified with
      \(\mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\).
  \end{enumerate}
  Once these two are available --- the present lemma is exactly their
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\)-level assembly --- the conclusion is immediate:
  \(g_x\) iso \(\Rightarrow \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\) iso \(\Rightarrow\)
  \((F \triangleleft g)\) stalkwise iso \(\Rightarrow F \triangleleft g \in J.W\),
  hence locally injective.
  Tensoring an isomorphism \(g_x\) by the identity \(\mathrm{id}_{F_x}\) yields an
  isomorphism --- no flatness is needed, since \(g_x\) is invertible, not merely
  injective. Hence \(F \triangleleft g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, in particular
  locally injective.

The stalkwise computation rests on three ingredients with distinct status at the

  \begin{enumerate}

      the underlying \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map is an isomorphism. This is built
      project-side, axiom-clean, and furnishes the \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\)
      step below.
    \item[(d.1-bridge)] (remaining) the site-\(J.W\) \(\Longleftrightarrow\)
      stalkwise-iso characterisation on \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\): that a morphism lies
      in \(J.W\) iff its stalk maps are isomorphisms at every point. The concrete
      Mathlib assembly route uses the \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) structure
      together with the topological stalk criteria --- local injectivity
      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) injectivity on stalks and local surjectivity
      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) surjectivity on stalks --- bridging the site-level

  \(g_x\) is invertible, not merely injective. Hence \(F \triangleleft g\) is a
  stalkwise isomorphism, in particular locally injective.

The stalkwise computation rests on three ingredients with distinct status at the
  pinned commit:
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item[(d.1-done)] the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear stalk-map packaging
      (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\) and its companions, \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}):
      a morphism of presheaves of modules induces an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear
      map on stalks, characterised on germs, that becomes a linear equivalence once
      the underlying \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map is an isomorphism. This is built
      project-side, axiom-clean, and furnishes the \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step
      above.
    \item[(d.1-bridge)] (remaining) the site-\(J.W\) \(\Longleftrightarrow\)
      stalkwise-iso characterisation on \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\): that a morphism lies
      in \(J.W\) iff its stalk maps are isomorphisms at every point. The concrete
      Mathlib assembly route uses the \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) structure
      together with the topological stalk criteria --- local injectivity
      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) injectivity on stalks and local surjectivity
      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) surjectivity on stalks --- bridging the site-level
      \(\mathtt{Presheaf.IsLocallyInjective}\) / \(\mathtt{IsLocallySurjective}\)
      to the \(\mathtt{TopCat.Presheaf}\) stalk versions. This half is still
      Mathlib-absent at the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level.
    \item[(d.2)] (remaining) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
      \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk (a filtered colimit) with the relative
      module-presheaf tensor, under which \((F \triangleleft g)_x\) is identified
      with \(\mathrm{lTensor}\,F_x\,(g_x) = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\) (using
      that \(\mathtt{tensorLeft}\) / \(\mathtt{tensorRight}\) preserve filtered
      colimits over a module category). This is genuinely Mathlib-absent over a
      \emph{varying} ring and is the largest remaining piece.
  \end{enumerate}
  Mathlib supplies the analogues for presheaves valued in a fixed monoidal concrete
  category, but not for modules over a varying ring; porting (d.1-bridge) and (d.2)
  to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) is the remaining work. Once (d.2) lands, the
  conclusion is immediate and flatness-free: by
  \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\) the stalk map \(g_x\) is a
  linear equivalence, and the linear-equivalence \(\mathrm{lTensor}\) by \(F_x\)
  carries it to an isomorphism, finishing the proof.
